summary:
The Federalist Papers (1787–1788 AD) were a collection of 85 essays written by Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay under the pseudonym 'Publius,' arguing for ratification of the [[event:2396|US Constitution]] — the most important work of political theory produced in American history and one of the foundational texts of republican government worldwide. Written as newspaper essays in New York state (a key battleground for ratification) and collected in book form in 1788, the Federalist Papers responded to Anti-Federalist critics who feared that the Constitution would create a tyrannical central government. Hamilton wrote 51 essays, Madison 29, and Jay 5; Madison's contributions, particularly Federalist No. 10 (arguing that large republics are more resistant to faction than small ones, inverting conventional wisdom) and No. 51 (articulating the separation of powers and checks and balances), are considered the most intellectually profound.

The Federalist Papers have had a peculiar dual life: in their immediate context they were partisan documents arguing for a specific political outcome; in their subsequent legacy they became the authoritative commentary on constitutional intent, cited by American courts, scholars, and politicians across two and a half centuries. Madison's argument in Federalist No. 10 — that the diversity of a large republic would prevent any single faction from dominating, since coalitions would be fragile and shifting — was an explicit response to [[event:3264|Montesquieu's]] argument that republics could only function in small territories. The Papers drew on [[event:3291|Locke]], [[event:3264|Montesquieu]], [[event:3263|Rousseau]], and ancient history to argue that the Constitution represented the best available approximation of a government that would protect liberty while maintaining order — an argument that continues to resonate.
